comments.content:
In The Doctrine of High Magic: Chapter 16, you wrote the following:

"Magic is the result of will and imagination acting on the astral light, the subtle energy that links all minds and things together. If you direct your will and imagination toward some goal, that action will set a current moving in the astral light; if you confirm that direction of will and imagination through actions on the material plane—yes, we can refer to these actions as “rituals”—the current becomes fixed in place, and will infallibly have an effect, either on its target or on you. Once fixed in place, the current cannot be changed."

This made me think of something: the way that so often, when someone has a fear they cannot bring themselves to face, they twist themselves into weird shapes to avoid dealing with the fear, and then tolerably often that fear comes into reality in an extreme form. That fear is a strong emotion, the imagery is there somewhere in the subconscious (and thus the frantic effort to avoid it), and the efforts to avoid it are "rituals" in this sense. Is it possible that the reason that frantic efforts to avoid facing fears so often bring it into manifestation is a result of unconsciously doing magic?

replies.title: (no subject)

Wouldn't one be "tempted" to avoid specific kinds of difficult situations, thereby impairing one's ability to actually deal with them and make peace with their existence?
In other words, wouldn't this process of bringing magic into consciousness become a process of digging one's heels into whatever biases one already has?

And, more importantly, wouldn't this conscious magic, while bringing about less opportunity for low-level interference by non-physical beings, at the same time increase the chances for higher-level, more subtle and more pernicious entities?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Quite the contrary. First, to develop the capacity to do effective magic, it's necessary to develop a focused will and a great deal of self-knowledge, and the combination of these makes it much easier to recognize that this or that situation has to be confronted, and then to confront it. Biases in particular don't thrive in the presence of reflective self-knowledge.

Second, where do you get the idea that these subtle and pernicious entities you're discussing have any difficulty influencing unconscious magic? I recommend a good thorough reading of Jung. He makes the point, with a great deal of clinical experience to back it, that it's only by bringing unconscious forces into a working relationship with consciousness that these forces can be brought into balance.

Leaving your own magical capacities unconscious makes you a plaything of what he called the archetypes -- they have other names, of course. Waking up to your magical powers enables you to work with them creatively and constructively, rather than being passive to them.

replies.title: Re: On Karenism and anger
replies.content:
Yes, an actual transfer is definitely happening. It rather is like a buildup of stagnant energy, in the sense of energy getting stuck in a fixed (as in repeated/rehearsed) pattern, like the way an eddy can trap things floating in a stream, or an eddy in air currents can build into a tornado. Even though being exposed to the sufferer often feels more like uncontrolled, flailing chaos, her pattern itself is what has gotten stuck or stagnant. [By “her”, I’m referring to your Karen example, and by no means excluding men from the category of abusers of this universal coping mechanism.]

To temporarily alleviate the swirling, siphoning energy drain that is holding her captive, the sufferer lobs a chunk of that trapped pattern at any convenient victim. If the pre-existing obstacles within the targeted victim's energy flow — which all of us carry around in abundance — are aligned in a way that allows that trapped pattern to form a lasting eddy, then the sufferer has successfully transferred her energy siphon onto a new victim. By dumping part of her affliction on others, the sufferer will only enjoy a temporary abatement of symptoms, as her stuck pattern will rapidly grow back to its full force, requiring her to go back on the rampage. Your shaking the attack off and walking away with a smile leaves the eddy unattached and free to return home to its discontented owner, now turning an improbable shade of blue.

Pretty much any intolerable (read: unfelt) emotion can cause one's energy to get stuck in habitual, rehearsed patterns. Anger is certainly a favorite go to, but jealousy, envy, depression, and fear are every bit as able to fuel ongoing, siphoning energy drains. Joy, happiness, and contentment are less likely to turn into fixed burdens that their beneficiaries refuse to feel, but it does happen. If you've ever felt the desire to escape from the clutches of someone who demands that everyone around them must be endlessly happy, you've experienced what having frozen eddies of unfelt happiness dumped on you feels like. It's dreadful! Of course, like anger, brittle and explosive happiness is usually covering over far less tolerable emotions.

Another name for these fixed swirls is energy parasites. They can be astral, etheric, spiritual, or mental, depending on which energies they entrap and siphon off. Despite their each being made of a particular type of entwined energy, any of them can cause dreadful effects on all of the planes (a bit like us!) Often, when a sufferer’s rampage makes a whole place feel uncomfortable, she is trying to dump more of her trapped patterns onto the available victims than they are remotely vulnerable to absorbing. In those cases, all the budded eddies she’s hurling so enthusiastically at her targets go whizzing all over the place, getting lodged in unsuspecting bystanders and making them want to flee the mayhem. Now why am I suddenly reminded of family holiday get-togethers?

We are indeed all practicing magic all the time, for better or worse. Are we aware of what we’re doing? And are we at all proud of the results we’re effecting around us? While Karens might defiantly screech in the affirmative to both those questions, if they could experience just once a more effective way to resolve their suffering, they might finally be able to begin the long journey of questioning and discovery that awaits us all. Until then, just keep shaking vigorously and walk away with your smile intact.

— Christophe

replies.title: Re: Troubled history and stable present
replies.content: It's not a matter of conditions in the past, but rather of conditions in the future. Several times in history -- after the Christian seizure of power in the Roman world, for example, during the crusade against the Cathars, and at the end of the Renaissance -- a great deal of occult knowledge was lost because it had been kept secret by occult schools, and those were destroyed by persecution or (in the last case) simply deprived of funding and left to scrape by with too few students and resources to save everything. For close to a century and a half, a good many occultists have believed that we're heading into another such situation, but even more extreme, since we're facing the fall of a civilization. Thus the decision was made to get as much knowledge as possible into as many hands as possible, so that there's a much better chance that some of it will get through the troubled times ahead into the hands of the future. Yes, that's one of the reasons I've been putting effort into getting John Gilbert's teachings into circulation while there's still time and resources to do so.

replies.title: Re: Troubled history and stable present
replies.content:
The Picatrix could only get one killed in the Middle Ages because it was on the losing side of the war over which occult knowledge would get passed on when the Classical world fell apart. When the extraordinarily occult tradition of the early Christians (complete with secret symbols and handshakes) ended up winning that war, it viciously suppressed all its competitors. The future winner of the ideological wars we're currently gearing up for may not end up being anywhere near as obsessively jealous as Christianity was. On the other hand, the winner of this occult death-match could leave those Christians looking positively tolerant.

It is quite possible that one sole approach to magical practice will propagandize itself better than all other aspirants and then proceed to ruthlessly erase any traces of alternate approaches. Imagine a high Dark Age in which Crowleyism is the one true practice! Foolish religious holdouts may still mutter their forgotten rites in secret, always fearful of being revealed or discovered with a forbidden Bible, Veda, or Koran and burnt at the stake. Some may even remember archaic terms like Druidry and Shamanism, but no one will have any idea what their lost tenets or practices may have been. Travelers from far distant lands will tell of exotic rites exerting their own powerful magic, but all devout Crowleyians will simply pentagram themselves and turn away from such blasphemy.

We should try to get every form of magic, including religion, written down in as many places as possible. Our descendants could perhaps end up being more tolerant than anyone’s expecting, but, if history is anything to go by, they will probably be as susceptible as our ancestors were to great waves of culling and purging washing over them periodically. You're not likely to lose anywhere near as much of value if you simply bet on social primates behaving like primitive monkeys.

— Christophe

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Both of those are good magical choices, and of course you can combine them. More generally, it sounds as though you're taking the matter seriously and doing constructive work, and that is also a magical act -- remember that every action carried out with intention is a magical ritual!

I'd encourage you to take the journaling work you're doing very, very seriously. Most people come to relationships with a vast amount of emotional baggage, much of which is laid down in childhood; it's a commonplace in the old psychological literature, for example, that most men are subconsciously either looking for their mother or looking for someone as different from their mother as possible, while most women have the same sort of subconscious fixation on their father or his opposite.

Unconscious expectations, assumptions, and fixations can mess your love life over like nothing else. Bring them to the surface, think about them, and get clear on the difference between fantasies and realities, and you have a much better chance of relating to a potential lover as a human being and not a screen onto which you project subconscious contents.

I really do need to revise the Magic Monday FAQ, don't I? Both these should be covered there, but aren't.

1) You're quite correct that the Golden Section Fellowship and the Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose have a connection with AODA, and it's not just the Sphere of Protection. AODA and the FHR in its original form -- as well as the Order of Spiritual Alchemy and the Modern Order of Essenes -- was affiliated with the Universal Gnostic Church, and I learned them all from John Gilbert. It was quite common back in the day for senior initiates to establish Fellowships of their own in the same tradition, and that's what the GSF is -- a development of the same tradition focused on classical occultism, sacred geometry, earth mysteries, and a few of my other pet subjects. ;-) So AODA work can certainly be practiced from the same perspective; there are differences you'll have to keep in mind, but that's true of each of the branches of the Universal Gnostic tradition.

2) Each of the orders has a specific oracle that's central to its symbolism. For AODA, that's Ogham; for the GSF, it's the Sacred Geometry Oracle; for the FHR, it's tarot; for the Celtic Golden Dawn work, it's geomancy. I wrote the Coelbren book so that it would be plug-and-play for practitioners of the Celtic Golden Dawn work, but the only material affected by that is the stuff in back, relating the Coelbren letters to the tarot and the Tree of Life; you can certainly use it with the others. Since you're a student of the FHR, for that matter, you're supposed to master three different divination systems, type not specified; the Coelbren would be a good choice for one of these. Just remember that the material on the Tree of Life in the back of the Coelbren book isn't really compatible with the FHR Tree of Life material!

comments.title: Planetary hours?
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
the first ecosophian meeting of Germany took place last weekend (two attendants ;) Huzzah!

We were jointly wondering how "the ancients" figured out the planetary hours (encyclopedia of natural magig- style) without a working clock and somewhere to look up the lenghts of the day? Sundial and dead reckoning?
Thank you for your work.

replies.title: Re: Planetary hours?
replies.content:
The ancients, both medieval and earlier, had numerous devices for figuring out planetary hours. The Antikythera mechanism from ancient Greece was thought to have been used for predicting astronomical positions and eclipses. Armillray spheres were first developed in China back in the 4th century BC. There are any number of images from the Middle Ages of mural sextants used to measure star positions. It's really surprising the devices the ancients had at their disposal.

JLfromNH/Jaundice Erudite Ostrich

replies.title: Re: Planetary hours?
replies.content: As JL points out, the ancients had plenty of effective timepieces. If you know what day of the year it is you can measure time within a quarter hour or so from the angle of the sun, which you can determine by any number of convenient methods -- sextants, astrolabes, tall Egyptian obelisks with  markings on the pavement around it, and the list goes on. At night, you pay attention to the rising and setting of stars close to the celestial equator, or use your sextant, astrolabe, etc. to get the angle of a bright star. There were also water clocks, which gauge time by the rate at which water drips out of one jug into another, and in the East, sticks of incense were made using precise formulations so that they burned at a steady pace. By the early Middle Ages you had the first mechanical clocks -- Gerbert of Aurillac built one in the 10th century, and even before then there were mechanical gimmicks to tell the monk in charge of the prayer bell when to ring the bell for each of the monastic services.

replies.title: Unconditional Love
replies.content:
I can give my own understanding about the OP's first question. The one about unconditional love. This is how I explained it to my mother one day:

"the Ultimate's Love"/"God's/Goddess's Love" whatever you want to call it is unconditional because it's what's there when all the products of the impressions of Mind are swept and flowing clean and clear.

It's like gravity in a way. No matter whether you are a saint or worst sinner the planet has ever seen makes no difference at all into how gravity operates on both this planet and in this universe.

If a little kid got scared that whenever he or she did something bad that the planet would punish them for their specific misdeed by letting them float off into outer space to die you would know that little kid misunderstood entirely how gravity works. So it is with unconditional love.

That's why Jesus said God-the-Father makes no discrimination between the saint and the sinner. Unconditional Love doesn't operate like that because it operates outside the bounds of a normal, conditioned events of the human mind, emotions and energy patterns. So as a shortcut we say God's Love is Unconditional. Yes, in the same way gravity is unconditional and non-discriminatory between the greatest Saints and the greatest mass murderers on this planet.


But typically the only people who verify a "law of Universal Unconditioned Love" are people who engage in enough of certain kinds of sadhana (spiritual practices) to personally re-discover it (just like the myriad who came before them and the myriad who re-discover it after them). Just like it takes working in a lab to see first-hand the results of certain laws about the properties of chemical bonds, etc. you read about in your college physics textbook.



It's also why the universe's law of unconditional love doesn't give you a Get Out of Jail Free card from the consequences of your own deeds. Nor from the consequences of being oblivious or clueless to other's deeds that effect you either. Unconditional Love doesn't mean I escape the consequences of my stupid deeds nor of the need to be aware of the impact of other people's stupid or malicious deeds at or to me. Just like I can't escape the law of gravity even though I don't know how to write out the mathematical formula for it. I get shoved off a high cliff or out a high rise window by an angry, psychotic patient having a breakdown - well, I'm dead just the same because the law of gravity for that event was doing what it ever does. For everyday life gravity is pretty much "unconditional" too.


Thus the need to constantly upgrade one's perception, understanding and wisdom in order to be a more flourishing,thriving and hopefully increasingly talented person in whatever endeavors one chooses to explore and practice.


So JMG is quite right that the universe doesn't care if you make a mistake or "sin" or are a fantastically blessed saint in that regard. Unconditional Love remains untouched both by the saintliness of one's deeds (Be Not Thou Proud, Ceasar since the Universe has seen an infinity of your kind's saintly deeds before and will see an infinity of them again by others when you are dust...or as JMG might agree in saying, "You ain't Special, dude"). Nor of the seeming indifference it has to innocent people suffering at the hands of a torturing, murdering twit. Just like gravity isn't kerfluffled by the saint or murderous twit either.


Hope that helps. :)

replies.title: Asters
replies.content: If it is Asters you're seeing, they flower now because they need a certain level of day light to prompt blooming, as do many plants. In horticultural terms, they're photo-period dependent. Some plants need long hours of daylight to flower and others need a shorter period. Since asters flower late, when the nights are longer, it makes sense that it happens not long before the frosts begin. Saying that, I've been noticing unseasonal behaviour in plants for a few years now, such as Hellebore flowering in autumn rather than late winter.

comments.title: gods and pantheons
comments.content:
My musings (sorry for the length) lately have me trying to understand the line where gods meet the human mind, what they are exactly, and what it means when you said you were surprised the Mesopotamian gods were moving, as opposed to other pantheons.

The way I currently understand this is by picturing a vast sea, which is the combination of all the forces that the universe has. It is inaccessible to us in the same way that our senses make it impossible to directly access the reality beyond what our minds construct. We cannot go into the sea.

We are on land, which is the material world of humans and what the human mind is capable of. The gods as we understand them inhabit the liminal space between the sea and the land, a product of the interaction between the two, like what we perceive as reality in our minds not being so. That space is just a line in the sand. The gods do not exist as such but are the product of the sea crashing onto the shore in certain places, creating an effect which exists in all time. Human minds in our past interpreted the crashing waves by creating a pantheon to explain the incoming waves as they experienced them.

However, the smaller currents which are part of the waves for each force are countless, and whereas they are all part of larger currents of forces, they have different “flavours” when they crash onto specific shores. So the force for war, for example, is expressed in the human mind as a different god when it meets a certain “shore”, meaning that the god or goddess of war in one group of people isn't quite the same as the god or goddess of war in another group. But again, they don't exist except as the interaction between force and mind.

So why should you be surprised that the Mesopotamian gods are “moving”? It isn't the gods that are moving (the particular gods don't exist as such) so much as some humans are responding to incoming crashes by using the myths of that particular pantheon to understand them. The forces that the Mesopotamian gods represent exist in all time and can crash onto any shore at any time. The pantheons as we understand them are part of the astral light forever and can be used by anyone to understand the forces.

And then, to make things just a little more confusing, the spirits of the land here in Canada are not the same as the spirits of the land in ancient Mesopotamia. I'm wondering if the reason the Mesopotamian gods speak to me is because they are of a time older than the European gods and the cultures that produced them and may be closer to the flavour of the spirits that inhabit this land.

As an example, I asked you a while back about sacred mountains. Coincidentally (or probably not), a beautiful book about North American aboriginal sacred mountains came into my hands not long after and it dawned on me that the whisperings I am hearing are in fact the spirits of THIS land trying to break through to me but not using the aboriginal myths, which seem strange to me because they belong to a very different culture. In other words, the crashes coming in might be trying to find a way to speak to me using a synthesis of the European mind I inherited from my culture, a pantheon that is older than (and bypasses) the European cultures, the fact that I am a native of this land, and have many native women in my family tree which began here in Canada in 1632, which means I am also a child of the peoples of this land physically though not culturally. And I am trying to find a way to understand the crashes coming by using whatever I can that makes sense.

So my questions are: would you say I am on the right track in understanding all this? Or am I missing something? How can I work with the images of one pantheon to understand something very foreign, and more importantly, should I? Is this a very bad idea all round? What are the dangers of doing this?

Thank you so much for taking the time to listen to my ramblings! Any input you care to give is greatly appreciated.

-Myriam

replies.title: Re: Divination question
replies.content: In my experience, at least, there are many things that can cause muddy responses to oracles. If you've misunderstood the situation so that your question has no right answer, that's one cause; if you don't want to hear what the oracle is trying to tell you, that's one cause; if you don't know enough about the situation to understand the reading, that's one cause; if the situation is still inchoate and its outcome is impossible to determine yet, that's one cause, and so on. When I get such a response, I write down the details and, if I possibly can, take no action on the situation, other than trying to find out more about it. A few days later, especially if I then have a clear sense of what the problem was, I try again.

comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I'm slowly making my way through the back issues of your Magic Mondays, and came across your experiment using sharp iron to break the glamour of the internet devices a while ago. I read through the reports with interest, because I'd been fighting futilely against internet addiction for years. But the various incidents of phones and routers etc. breaking for good scared me off. I didn't want to lose my devices (I'm too poor to buy replacements at the drop of a hat), because I still need them (for banking, for example, or connecting to dreamwidth) - I just wanted more control over my use of them.

So I remembered a bit of folklore about Rowan breaking fairy glamour, and the similarities of internet algorithms and fairy mischief seemed similar enough to me to try it out. I took a Rowan leaf and taped it onto the back of my phone.

It worked amazingly well! The pull to take it out and use it incessantly went away, and I was even able to leave it in my backpack while driving to work, and leave it there the whole day until I came home in the evening.

After a while, I noticed that the old bad habits came creeping back, though. When I checked the Rowan leaf, it had wilted (a process that had been slowed down by the clear tape covering it) and even partly torn off. Since it was already October, and the leaves yellowing on the trees anyway, I tried to come up with an alternative that would help me through the winter. So I took three shining red berries, cut them in half to make them less bulky, and taped those to the back of my phone. That was a week ago.

Today, out of the blue, the phone stopped working, and nothing I've tried so far has brought it back. It looks as if I'll have to replace it. (No juice seeping through the back or anything else material that would've caused it to suddenly shut down.)

I'm wondering what to try next. Return to using the leaf? Carry some berries on my person instead of glueing them to the phone? Or I could just believe in coincidence, I guess. But when a single leaf helps me not to text while driving anymore*), I'm inclined to take it more seriously than as just a coincidence.

*) I clearly recognized that habit as being hazardous to my own life, that of my dogs (who I take to work with me), and that of other people; but I was unable to break with it, and what was worse, I was unable to have an emotional reaction to that realization. Normally, it should've scared me. Even realizing that my complete lack of emotional reaction wasn't normal didn't elicit an emotional reaction to THAT realization. It was at that point that it dawned on me that something was SERIOUSLY wrong. I stopped with that insane behavior as soon as I had taped the leaf to the phone, without any struggle or relapse, and what was even more striking, I was able to react with the appropriate horror to my former behavior.

comments.title: Antipsychotic medication and magic
comments.content:
Some years ago, in the aftermath of an LSD trip I went psychotic and did some bad stuff that got me in legal trouble. I was prescribed antipsychotics and some time after that, with psychiatric supervision, went off them, since the original bout was caused by LSD. After some months off meds, I experienced another psychosis, and went on antipsychotics again. Are there magical/occult alternatives to dealing with psychosis?

replies.title: Re: Antipsychotic medication and magic
replies.content:
It's a piece of standard advice in occult schools that if somebody's on antipsychotics, and can't get off them without having a psychotic episode, they need to leave magic strictly alone until they're off the meds and mentally stable. I'd encourage you to follow that advice. First get your psyche functioning in a stable manner, then consider magic.

Trying to do magic if you have psychiatric problems is like trying to take up athetics when you haven't recovered from a physical injury -- you can mess yourself up very thoroughly. Don't go there.

comments.title: Meditation for Kids
comments.content:
JMG (and anyone else),

Any suggestions on a good way to get my 11 year old son into meditation?

Currently started doing simple HeadSpace app mindfulness meditation with him (I know it's limited but mostly just to get him used to sitting still and not fidgeting and not moving and some basic mental discipline) and can then move him on to other stuff.

I've read JMG's Discursive Meditation posts, but not sure he's quite ready for all of that, so wondering where to take it with him.

He lacks focus and his attention jumps around a lot and he's constantly moving and can be a real "space cadet" except on stuff he's interested in. I have never had him formally assessed or diagnosed as I am determined not to medicalize the issue, but several teachers have said they think he might have ADHD, but I certainly want to help him calm his mind and learn to focus better and am looking for suggestions on how to help with that (not wanting medical advice re ADHD just to be clear).

He is an avid competitive boxer (junior level) and also plays a lot of soccer and is very sporty (and is invariably praised by his various coaches and told something like "you're very talented but you really need to focus better and be more disciplined about training to get to the next level") and is basically good at any sport he tries (when we took him for a couple of ice skating lessons, we were randomly approached by a coach at the ice rink and asked if she could train him because he had the potential to be a serious competitor; when he had his first tennis lesson, the coach found it hard to believe it was his first ever lesson etc).

Academically though he is bored out of his gourd and stares off into space in most of his classes and infuriates his teachers because it looks like he's ignoring them when he's not even hearing them etc (but he's focussed and enjoys it when - for example - I teach him history or complex political issues like lockdowns in the context of engaging stories).

Anyway, I might post in an Open Thread on the other blog for more general comments, but here I'd be particularly interested in any advice on getting him into meditation or other allied mental disciplines that could help him calm himself down and focus (in particular he's interested in meditative or mental practices that could help him with his boxing and how to get into a meditative flow state of awareness and Zen in the midst of combat, because I told him the best martial artists operate like that - pure awareness - and in fact the day I told him that, his meditation improved dramatically because he really made an effort).

replies.title: Re: Meditation for Kids
replies.content: If you want to continue in the eastern tradition, "The Attention Revolution" by Alan Wallace is probably one of the better books out there. The discursive meditation given by our host is a really good option though, and one that deserves serious consideration. It is probably the most well rounded and robust method that I've been exposed to. Finally, a technique called "Kim's Game" is excellent. I've only practiced it as an adult, but I know several people who were taught it as children, with fantastic results.

replies.title: Re: Meditation for Kids
replies.content:
First, you might consider getting him out of the public schools and putting him into some program that will let him use his mind, or homeschooling him. I got the same kind of pushback from my teachers for the same reason. He's bored out of his gourd because the public schools are monumentally boring: a mental wasteland for anyone with an IQ noticeably higher than a turnip's. Getting him into a situation in which he can learn at his own speed could solve a lot of problems for him.

Second, any basic kind of meditation, like the one suggested by u/taylorrose, would be a good choice. Discursive meditation isn't going to help him with his boxing, so something like zazen might be a better option. If your son's interested in expanding his combat arts background, one of the martial arts that includes meditation practice might be a very good choice, but of course that depends on where you are.

Third, "Kim's Game" is wonderful mind training. Rudyard Kipling's novel Kim is the best source for it.

replies.title: Re: Meditation for Kids
replies.content:
One further, less standard thing to consider: miniature or board wargames and roleplaying games provide a fantastic excuse to learn about history, politics, strategy, and more. I did fine in school, but I hated it, and it was only when I was reading a book on medieval history for fun thanks to D&D that I realized "I like learning, I just hate school".

On the other hand, I hated sports at the time and was a bookish nerd, so they might not be as much help for your athletically gifted son!

Cheers,
Jeff

comments.title: Ashtanga yoga and Western Esoteric Practices
comments.content:
Good morning JMG, thank you for being here for us! If I may, I would like to respond to Ataulfo’s question last week. It was posted after my bedtime :)

Yes, Ashtanga vinyasa yoga has very precise breath work. One movement, one breath. In fact the only way to survive the 90 minutes of constant motion in the complete Primary Series is to strictly adhere to the breath work 😉

I practiced Ashtanga for 10 years and have been teaching the Primary Series for several more…after 10 years of old fashioned Iyengar yoga. I also am a couple of years into GD practice as taught by our esteemed host.

To practice the two together regularly and properly is A LOT. If you are already into an esoteric practice, add the Ashtanga carefully and pay strict attention to your energetic body (it can get whooped!) and diet (meat included several times a week). I wouldn’t recommend starting both practices together at the same time, rather begin one, persevere for a few months then add the other.

Ashtanga is an excellent way to build focus, stamina and will. But keep in mind Patabi Jois designed the vinyasa for young men in their late teens and twenties. Currently I am teaching a dialed back version focusing on breath work, etheric energy flow, balance and gracefulness. I make it more or less demanding depending on which students are at practice. But we skip all the mad jump backs, jump forwards and a lot of chaturangas…too hard on the wrists! That seems to work for me.

Honestly I also teach a beginners Iyengar class that would fit the bill better for combination with Western esoteric practice until one is really strong and up to speed. There we focus on breathing, mindfulness, posture and getting to know our energetic bodies…..and I sneak in a fair workout too!

Very best of luck with your endeavors! SundaraYogaShala

replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Ashtanga yoga and Western Esoteric Practices
replies.content:
Hi Ataulfo, If you like an intense practice you will really enjoy Ashtanga! It can be addictive so be careful to mind your limits….slowly slowly.

Well…..in the real world Ashtanga seems to be all over the place from the preening egoists seeking those buns of steel to the few who do indeed deepen their practice through study of the Sutras and Indian philosophy, which can make the practice very profound. Make sure to have an ishta devata to guide and protect your practice…Ganesha is mine and he is totally on board with my western esoteric work so that blends nicely as well.

I can see that my abrahamic, monotheistic upbringing sets me up for that; and to ask a different way - in a world full of Gods, how does one choose whom to worship while ignoring the rest? I recognize that it is a personal issue; and, I feel there’s a more broad issue in that so many of the deities of antiquity are described as jealous and/or easily offended.

I recognize that specific deities can become deeper, personal relationships, and these are naturally the places where most religious practice and prayer would be directed. In studying ancient cultures and polytheistic practices, it also seems there’s quite a bit of “covering the bases” i.e. making offerings to certain Gods, even if they are not personal or familial patrons, but just because they are powerful and/or take offense. Like making offerings to Hestia before any other offerings. We have much more knowledge in modern times of all the Gods and Goddesses of antiquity. Is there merit in being fearful of accidentally offending the multitudes of deities?

Reading through myths, there is so much violence,, jealousy, and worse perpetrated by the Gods. I've been drawn to pantheons and practices that are (somewhat) better documented; but perhaps all the documentation and stories serve more to divide me from the deities as they are currently active in our world. Is one of the draws of the Welsh deities perhaps that they have so little documentation, so there is less assumed and more room for relationship building? Also, though there's less info to draw you to them, there's also less info to trigger caution. (For example, with norse deities, I worry that bringing their energy into my life encourages more conflict.)

I wonder at how much of my concern about this is a "first-world problem." It seems like the proliferation of differing beliefs is enabled by wealth concentration and peoples' ability to live separate from a community. So, (as with trying to pick a product at the overstuffed grocers) I'm spoiled for choice and worried about the opportunity cost of picking a direction.

My apologies for the meandering thoughts and interspersed questions. I've been pondering and meditating on these topics and finding myself hitting a wall. Perhaps its best to just step away from the religious side for a bit and focus on magical practice without deities. Perhaps some greater focus on OSA work, sacred geometry, and time in nature might help me work through whatever it is that causes me so much stress around this.

Thank you for your time.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Yes, it's a first world problem. It's also generally a matter of overliteralism. We don't know much about the reality behind the gods and goddesses of mythology; we know that something's there, we know that it or they respond when approached by traditional practices such as prayer and ritual, and that's about it. Are the Greek gods, the Norse gods, the Welsh gods, etc. actually separate from one another? Ultimately, we don't know; it may be that there's just one thunder god, for example, who interacts with various people in various ways, or there may be lots of thunder gods.

If you're getting stressed out about deities, let go of the issue and do other things for a while. The gods are patient; they don't stress out about you!

comments.content:
I've had a somewhat creepy thought lately, that I can't quite shake, and would like to know if this sounds plausible to you. The traditional lore has it that demons get your soul as a plaything between incarnations if you make the mistake of selling your soul to them, from which it follows that the longer you'll be out of incarnation, the more valuable it is for them to buy your soul, while the shorter gap between lives, the less valuable it'll be. This also makes an uncomfortable sense of the way that whenever they get strong, they tend to encourage truly massive catastrophes and many of our collective interactions with them end with a huge body count: this results in the loss of a lot of bodies, and thus increases the number of souls out of incarnation and therefore the length of time between incarnations. It also explains their obsession with infanticide, abortion, and non-procreative sex, given that all of these reduce the number of bodies available for souls to incarnate into, and thus increases the time between incarnations.

From this, it follows that during times when souls are out of incarnation longer, the demons will get more when they acquire someone's soul, and so will be more motivated to try for it the longer they expect a soul to be out of incarnation; while a soul for someone who will be out of incarnation for only a few years will be worth less to them. From this, it follows that they should be more active when they expect souls to be out of incarnation longer, and less active when the souls will be out for shorter lengths of time.

All of this is interesting in light of the fact that we may have reached the peak of our population, and the gaps between incarnations, which have likely been getting shorter, are about to get longer again. However, what troubles me is that sharp uptick in demonic activity over the past few years: some of this may be human stupidity, but there was plenty of that before, and this is also the pattern I'd expect to see if there's about to be a sharp increase in how long souls remain out of incarnation, such as because of a mass die off, or a dramatic reduction in fertility; both of which seem to be likely to occur as a result of a certain ongoing medical experiment.

Does this line of reasoning seem plausible to you?

replies.content: Unpleasaantly plausible, yes. The one thing I'd point out is that demonic beings were also very active during, say, the 1930s -- the Nazis had a lot of dealings with them -- even though most people were going to be out of incarnation fairly briefly.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Global population was less than 3 billion then, compared to over 7 billion now. This would suggest that the gap between incarnations was probably less then than it is now, but I'd need to do research I'm not sure how to perform to say for sure.

replies.title: Re: Western Esoteric Tradition and Ancestors/Ancestor Worship
replies.content: I get asked this from time to time, and the answer is "nope." Ancestor worship is not a part of the Western esoteric tradition. About the closest you get is reverence for the saints among Christian occultists.

comments.title: Astral light
comments.content: Greetings JMG, reading about the astral light reminds me of the way the Holy Spirit is said to flow through and energize believers. One belief of the church (at least on the more traditional end) is the ability to increase and direct this flow through prayer, ritual, and other acts. As I meditate on this further, should I consider the Holy Spirit as *the* astral light, a ray or portion of the astral light, or a different flow all entirely?

replies.title: Re: Astral light
replies.content: Well, I'm not a specialist in Christian theology, but my understanding is that the Holy Spirit is the persona (literally, mask or manifestation) of the Christian god that works in and through the astral light. It's not the astral light, any more than a fish is the sea in which it swims, but it is present and active on the astral plane, just as Jesus was present and active on the material plane during his incarnate life.
